summary: This notebook demonstrates an inference pipeline for the BirdCLEF 2025 competition that combines two pre-trained Sound Event Detection (SED) models via a weighted blend. It processes 60-second audio clips into 5-second segments, extracts Mel spectrograms, and passes them through custom attention-based architectures built on eca_nfnet_l0 and seresnext26t_32x4d. The pipeline applies targeted post-processing techniques, including a power transformation for low-confidence predictions and temporal smoothing across adjacent segments, to generate the final submission. The author highlights that while this approach achieves a high public leaderboard score, it risks overfitting to the test set, a common pitfall in this competition. Readers should take away the value of strategic model blending and domain-aware post-processing for audio classification tasks.
competition_flow: Loads test audio files, extracts 5-second Mel spectrogram segments, runs them through two pre-trained SED models, applies power transformation and temporal smoothing post-processing, and blends the outputs with a 75/25 weight to produce a CSV submission.
data_reading: Reads .ogg files from test_soundscapes/ using torchaudio.load and soundfile, converting stereo audio to mono.
data_processing: Computes Mel spectrograms (32kHz SR, 1024 FFT, 512 hop, 128 mels, HTK scale).
Applies log transform and per-segment standard deviation normalization.
Uses AmplitudeToDB (top_db=80) for one model variant.
Applies power transformation to low-confidence prediction columns.
Applies temporal smoothing via weighted neighbor averaging across segments.
ensembling: Combines two model outputs using a weighted average (75% seresnext, 25% nfnet), followed by a power transformation on low-confidence predictions and temporal smoothing across adjacent 5-second segments.
insights: Strategic model blending can significantly boost leaderboard scores.
Applying power transformations to low-confidence predictions improves detection rates.
Temporal smoothing enforces realistic prediction consistency across audio segments.
High leaderboard scores in BirdCLEF often indicate overfitting to the public test set.
critical_findings: The weighted blend likely overfits to the public leaderboard test set, a recurring pattern in BirdCLEF competitions.
